content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

June 17, 2008

Stephen A. Holsinger
City Manager
City of Willows
201 North Lassen
Willows, California 95988

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-08-063

Dear Mr. Holsinger:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of the members of the Greater Willows Improvement League and Heather Baker, a member of the city council of the city of Willows, regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090. We urge you to seek appropriate legal advice to determine if these or any other laws outside the Act apply.

QUESTIONS

1. May a board member of the Greater Willows Improvement League, an unincorporated association acting as an advisory group to the City of Willows regarding a Façade Improvement Program in the Historic Downtown Commercial District, apply for funding from the City of Willows under this program for his or her own business or real property?

2. Do the Act's conflict-of-interest rules restrict board members of the Greater Willows Improvement League in making recommendations to the city regarding the Façade Improvement Program?

_______________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-08-063
Page No. 2

3. May Councilmember Heather Baker, who regularly attends meetings of the
Greater Willows Improvement League but holds no official position with the League, and
owns a business in the Historic Downtown Commercial District, participate in city
council decisions involving the Façade Improvement Program?

CONCLUSION

1. The Act does not prohibit board members of the Greater Willows
Improvement League from applying for funding under the Façade Improvement Program.

2. The Act's conflict-of-interest rules only apply to "public officials" as defined
in Section 82048. Because the Greater Willows Improvement League is not a state or
local government agency, its board members are not "public officials" at this point and
may make recommendations to the city on the Façade Improvement Program. However,
as discussed below, if the city regularly approves the League's substantive
recommendations over an extended period of time, the League's board members will
become public officials subject to the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions, meaning the
board members may be restricted in whether or how they make their recommendations.

3. If a city council decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial
effect on Councilmember Baker's business, she may not make, participate in making, or
otherwise use her position to influence that decision.

FACTS

The City of Willows recently endorsed the formation of an unincorporated
association, Greater Willows Improvement League ("GWIL"), to serve as an ad hoc
advisory group regarding a Façade Improvement Program in the city's Historic
Downtown Commercial District (the "District"). Two city council members regularly
attend GWIL meetings, but do not hold any official position on GWIL's board of
directors. The proposed Façade Improvement Program will be funded by the city. Under
the program, business owners/operators within the District will apply for grants. The city
council will have sole authority for approving any such grants. Most of GWIL's board
members own or operate businesses in the District and may seek to obtain a grant under
the Façade Improvement Program. One of the two council members noted above,
Councilmember Heather Baker, owns and operates a business in the District. You
advised in our telephone conversation that Councilmember Baker leases real property in
the District for her business under a month-to-month tenancy. GWIL's board and
Councilmember Baker have asked you to seek the Commission's advice on their behalf.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).)

File No. A-08-063
Page No. 3

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest. Thus, the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions do not
prevent a public official from acting in his or her private capacity, but instead restrict the
official's actions in his or her official capacity. Therefore, the Act's conflict-of-interest
provisions do not prevent a board member of the GWIL, even if it's determined GWIL
board members are public officials, from applying for or receiving funding from the city
under the Façade Improvement Program. However, if they are public officials, the Act
may restrict their official decisionmaking on the program.

The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether
an official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation 18700(b).) The general
rule, however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes a
governmental decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one
or more of his or her financial interests.

GWIL Board Members

Step 1. Are GWIL board members "public officials" within the meaning of
Section 87100?

Section 82048 defines a public official as "every member, officer, employee or
consultant of a state or local government agency." The term "public official" is further
defined by Regulation 18701(a)(1):

"(a) For purposes of Government Code section 82048,
which defines 'public official,' and Government Code
section 82019, which defines 'designated employee,' the
following definitions apply:

"(1) 'Member' shall include, but not be limited to, salaried
or unsalaried members of committees, boards or
commissions with decisionmaking authority."

Under Regulation 18701(a)(1)(A) a board or commission possesses decision
making authority whenever:

"(i) It may make a final governmental decision;

"(ii) It may compel a governmental decision; or it may
prevent a governmental decision either by reason of an
exclusive power to initiate the decision or by reason of a
veto which may not be overridden; or

"(iii) It makes substantive recommendations which are, and

File No. A-08-063
Page No. 4

over an extended period of time have been, regularly
approved without significant amendment or modification
by another public official or governmental agency."

If the GWIL board of directors meets any of the tests of Regulation 18701(a)(1)(A)(i),
(ii) or (iii), its members would be considered public officials who are subject to the Act's
conflict-of-interest rules.

The facts you have provided indicate that GWIL does not make, compel or
prevent governmental decisions as contemplated by Subdivisions (a)(1)(A)(i) or (ii).
However, members of advisory bodies may nonetheless be considered public officials if
the requirements of Subdivision (a)(1)(A)(iii) are met. This requires an assessment of the
impact of an advisory body's recommendations by analyzing the extent to which its
recommendations have been followed in the past.

If the recommendations of a body have a significant impact on the ultimate
outcome of a decision, then the body is considered to have decisionmaking authority and
is not solely advisory. (In re Rotman (1987) 10 FPPC Ops. 1.) In the past, the
Commission has advised that newly formed advisory bodies that have no history of
recommendations being regularly approved over an extended period of time are not yet
decision making entities under the Act. (Wiener Advice Letter, No. A-06-118; Michelon
Advice Letter, No. I-94-224; Wilson Advice Letter, No. I-94-307; Cader-Thompson
Advice Letter, No. A-97-393; Loomis Advice Letter, No. A-99-295.) Once there is a
history of a particular advisory body's recommendations being routinely accepted
without amendment or modification, the body converts from a solely advisory function to
one of making a governmental decision, and its members are considered public officials
subject to the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act. (Wiener, supra).

Your letter states that the City of Willows recently endorsed the formation of
GWIL. It appears that GWIL has not yet made recommendations to the city council.
Accordingly, there is no history of recommendations being routinely accepted without
amendment or modification so as to convert GWIL from a solely advisory function to one
of making governmental decisions. Its board members, therefore, are not considered
public officials and are not subject to the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions.

Councilmember Baker

Step 1. Is Councilmember Baker a "public official" within the meaning of
Section 87100?

As a member of the Willows City Council, which is a local government agency,
Councilmember Baker is a public official. Therefore, she may not make, participate in
making, or otherwise use her position to influence any decisions that will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any of her economic interests.

File No. A-08-063
Page No. 5

**Step 2. Will Councilmember Baker be making, participating in making or
influencing a governmental decision?**

A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) A public official
"participates in a governmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his or her
position and without significant substantive or intervening review, the official
advises, or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker regarding the governmental
decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision if, for the purpose of influencing the
decision, the official contacts or appears before or otherwise attempts to influence, any
member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.3.)

Councilmember Baker will be making or participating in making governmental
decisions when, as a city councilmember, she participates in action items involving the
Façade Improvement Program. While the only decision identified in your letter is a city
council decision to award Façade Improvement funds to applicants, you have raised the
broader question of whether Councilmember Baker may participate in any decision
involving the Façade Improvement Program. Each governmental decision must be
analyzed individually. Accordingly, we limit our advice to the decision to award funds
and provide general guidance to assist with the analysis for all other governmental
decisions.

**Step 3. What are Councilmember Baker's economic interests?**

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts of interest arising
from certain enumerated *economic interests*. These economic interests are described in

**Section 87103 and Regulations 18703-18703.5, inclusive:**

• A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he
or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a);
Regulation 18703.1(a).)

• A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or
she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management.
(Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703.1(b).)

• A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she
has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b); Regulation
18703.2.)

File No. A-08-063
Page No. 6

• A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, totaling $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section
87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.)

• A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her
if the gifts total $390 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e);
Regulation 18703.4.)

• A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family. This is
commonly referred to as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103;
Regulation 18703.5.)

Councilmember Baker has an economic interest in the business she owns and
operates in the District because she is a "director, officer, partner, trustee or employee" of
the business. (Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703.1(b).) Presumably she also has an
investment of $2,000 or more in that business entity which gives rise to an economic
interest in the business entity. (Section 87103(a); Regulation 18703.1(a).) She does not
have an economic interest in the real property she leases for her business because under
Regulation 18233 the interest of a tenant in a periodic tenancy of one month or less is not
an interest in real property. Finally, if she receives income from her business or clients of
her business totaling $500 or more within 12 months prior to making or participating in a
governmental decision, she will also have an economic interest in the business and such
clients as a source of income. (Section 87103(e); Regulation 18703.4.)

Step 4. Will Councilmember Baker's economic interests be directly or
indirectly involved in decisions she will make, participate in making or influence as
a public official?

Under Regulation 18704.1(a) a person, including business entities and sources of
income, is directly involved in a decision before an official's agency when that person,
either directly or by an agent:

"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing
an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;

"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding
concerning the decision before the official or the official's
agency. A person is the subject of a proceeding if a
decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or
revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or
contract with, the subject person."

If Councilmember Baker applies for a Façade Improvement grant for her
business, her economic interest would be directly involved in the governmental decision
under Regulation 18704.1(a)(1).

File No. A-08-063
Page No. 7

Other governmental decisions regarding the Façade Improvement Program would
also need to be analyzed by applying Regulation 18704 to determine if her economic
interest will be directly involved in these decisions. Business entities and sources of
income that are not directly involved in governmental decisions under the rules quoted
above are regarded as indirectly involved. (Regulation 18704.1(b).)

Step 5. What is the applicable materiality standard?

A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial
effect of a governmental decision on a public official's economic interest is material.
(Regulation 18700(a).) Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on an economic interest will be material, depending on the
nature of the economic interest and whether that interest is directly or indirectly involved
in the agency's decision.

Under Regulation 18705.1(b)(1), the financial effects of a governmental decision
on a business entity that is directly involved in the governmental decision is presumed to
be material.² Furthermore, Regulation 18705.3(a) provides that any reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on a person who is a source of income to a public official and
who is directly involved in a decision before the official's agency is deemed material.
Accordingly, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the city council decision regarding
Councilmember Baker's application for Façade Improvement funds will have any effect
on her business, even a penny's worth, the effect is deemed material.

Business entities and sources of income that are not directly involved in
governmental decisions under the rules quoted above are subject to the materiality
standards for indirectly involved business entities under Regulation 18705.1(c).

Step 6. Is it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of the
governmental decision on Councilmember Baker's economic interest will meet that
applicable materiality standard?

An effect is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if the effect is "substantially
likely." (Regulation 18706; In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) Whether the
financial consequences of a governmental decision are substantially likely at the time the
decision is made depends on the specific facts surrounding the decision. (In re Thorner,
supra.) A financial effect need not be a certainty to be considered reasonably
foreseeable. On the other hand, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably
foreseeable.

² The presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the
governmental decision will have any financial effect, not even "one penny", on the business entity. This is
commonly referred to as the "one penny rule."

File No. A-08-063
Page No. 8

It is reasonably foreseeable that if Councilmember Baker's business receives
funds from the Façade Improvement Program, the decision to grant such funds will affect
her business by at least one penny. Accordingly, Councilmember Baker has a
disqualifying conflict of interest in the decision to grant such funds to her business.³

Steps 7 & 8. The "public generally" and "legally required participation"
exceptions.

Even if a material financial effect on a public official's economic interest is
reasonably foreseeable, he or she still may not be disqualified if the financial effect of the
governmental decision on the public official's economic interest is indistinguishable from
its effect on the public generally (Section 87103, Regulations 18700(b)(7) and 18707(a)),
or if the official is legally required to participate (Section 87103; Regulation 18708).
You have not presented any facts indicating that either of these exceptions is applicable
to the situation you have described.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: Valentina Joyce
     Counsel, Legal Division

VJ:jgl

_______________________________
³ When a public official who holds an office specified in Section 87200 has a conflict of interest
in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or she must: (1) immediately prior to the discussion of the
item, identify the type of economic interest involved in the decision as well as details of the
economic interest, as discussed in Regulation 18702.5(b), on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself
or herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. For closed
sessions, consent calendars, absences and speaking as a member of the public regarding personal interests,
special rules found in Regulation 18702.5(c) and 18702.5(d) apply.

effect of a governmental decision on a public official's economic interest is material.
(Regulation 18700(a).) Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on an economic interest will be material, depending on the
nature of the economic interest and whether that interest is directly or indirectly involved
in the agency's decision.
Under Regulation 18705.1(b)(l), the financial effects of a governmental decision
on a business entity that is directly involved in the governmental decision is presumed to
be material.' Furthermore, Regulation 18705.3(a) provides that any reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on a person who is a source of income to a public official and
who is directly involved in a decision before the official's agency is deemed material.
Accordingly, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the city council decision regarding
Councilmember Baker's application for Fagade Improvement funds will have any effect
on her business, even a penny's worth, the effect is deemed material.
Business entities and sources of income that are not directly involved in
govemmental decisions under the rules quoted above are subject to the materiality
standards for indirectly involved business entities under Regulation 18705.1(c).
Step 6. Is it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of the
governmental decision on Councilmember Baker's economic interest will meet that
applicable materiality standard?
An effect is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if the effect is "substantially
likely." (Regulation 18706; In re Thorner (1975) I Fppc ops. l9g.) whether the
financial consequences of a governmental decision are substantially likely at the time the
decision is made depends on the specific facts surrounding the decision. (ln re Thorner,
supra.) A financial effect need not be a certainty to be considered reasonably
foreseeable. On the other hand, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably
foreseeable.
2 The presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably fbreseeable that the
governmental decision will have any financial effect, not even "one penny", on the business entity. This is
commonly referred to as the "one penny rule."
It is reasonably foreseeable that if Councilmember Baker's business receives
funds from the Fagade Improvement Program, the decision to grant such funds will affect
her business by at least one penny. Accordingly, councilmember Baker has a
disqualifying conflict of interest in the decision to grant such funds to her business.3
steps 7 & 8. The "public generally" and "legally required participation,,
exceptions.
Even if a material financial effect on a public official's economic interest is
reasonably foreseeable, he or she still may not be disqualified if the financial effect of the
governmental decision on the public official's economic interest is indistinguishable from
its effect on the public generally (Section 87103, Regulations 18700(b)(7) and 18707(a)),
or if the official is legally required to participate (Section 87103; Regulation 18708).
You have not presented any facts indicating that either of these exceptions is applicable
to the situation you have described.

QUESTION: l. May a board member of the Greater Willows Improvement League, an
unincorporated association acting as an advisory group to the City of Willows regarding a
Faqade Improvement Program in the Historic Downtown Commercial District, apply for
funding from the City of Willows under this program for his or her own business or real
property?
2. Do the Act's conflict-of-interest ntles restrict board members of the Greater
Willows Improvement League in making recommendations to the city regarding the
Fagade Improvement Program?
' The Political Refbrm Act is contained in Government Code Sections 8 1000 through 91014. All
statutory ref'erences are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated.All regulatory ref'erences are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of
Regulations. unless otherwise indicated.


File No. ,4-08-063
3. May Councilmember Heather Baker, who regularly attends meetings of the
Greater Willows Improvement League but holds no official position with the League, and
owns a business in the Historic Downtown Commercial District, participate in city
council decisions involving the Fagade Improvement program?

CONCLUSION: 1. The Act does not prohibit board members of the Greater Willows
Improvement League from applying for funding under the Fagade Improvement program.
2. The Act's conflict-of-interest rules only apply to "public officials" as defined
in Section 82048. Because the Greater Willows tmplovemeri L.ugu" is not a state or
local govemment agency, its board members ure noi "public officiils" at this point and
may make recommendations to the city on the Fagade Improuement program. Ho*"r"r,
as discussed below, if the city regularly approves the League's substantive
recommendations over an extended period of time, the League's board members will
become public officials subject to the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions, meaning the
board members may be restricted in whether or how they makelheir recommendations.
3. If a city council decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial
effect on Councilmember Baker's business, she may not make, participate in making, or
otherwise use her position to influence that decision.
embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 June 17, 2008 Stephen A. Holsinger City Manager City of Willows 201 North Lassen Willows, California 95988 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-08-063 Dear Mr. Holsinger: This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of the members of the Greater Willows Improvement League and Heather Baker, a member of the city council of the city of Willows, regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090. We urge you to seek appropriate legal advice to determine if these or any other laws outside the Act apply. QUESTIONS 1. May a board member of the Greater Willows Improvement League, an unincorporated association acting as an advisory group to the City of Willows regarding a Façade Improvement Program in the Historic Downtown Commercial District, apply for funding from the City of Willows under this program for his or her own business or real property? 2. Do the Act's conflict-of-interest rules restrict board members of the Greater Willows Improvement League in making recommendations to the city regarding the Façade Improvement Program? _______________ ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-08-063 Page No. 2 3. May Councilmember Heather Baker, who regularly attends meetings of the Greater Willows Improvement League but holds no official position with the League, and owns a business in the Historic Downtown Commercial District, participate in city council decisions involving the Façade Improvement Program? CONCLUSION 1. The Act does not prohibit board members of the Greater Willows Improvement League from applying for funding under the Façade Improvement Program. 2. The Act's conflict-of-interest rules only apply to "public officials" as defined in Section 82048. Because the Greater Willows Improvement League is not a state or local government agency, its board members are not "public officials" at this point and may make recommendations to the city on the Façade Improvement Program. However, as discussed below, if the city regularly approves the League's substantive recommendations over an extended period of time, the League's board members will become public officials subject to the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions, meaning the board members may be restricted in whether or how they make their recommendations. 3. If a city council decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on Councilmember Baker's business, she may not make, participate in making,
